SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
why: The issue-resolution benchmark SWE Atlas defines itself 'beyond'; the foundational coding-agent eval.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
why: Codex + HumanEval — origin of functional-correctness code generation evaluation; most-cited reference.

title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
why: Established the agent-computer interface paradigm behind modern coding-agent scaffolds.

title: OpenHands: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents
why: Widely-used open agent platform for software engineering; common baseline scaffold.

title: RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code
why: Direct analog to the SWE Atlas Refactoring split — repository-level refactor evaluation.

title: HealthBench: Evaluating Large Language Models Towards Improved Human Health
why: OpenAI's physician-built health eval — most-cited reference and a flagship expert-rubric benchmark.

title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
why: OpenAI benchmark of agents on real Kaggle ML-engineering tasks; canonical AI-research-agent eval.

title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research
why: OpenAI eval of agents reproducing ICML papers from scratch with rubric grading — research-replication frontier.

title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
why: FutureHouse benchmark of practical biology-research tasks; precedent for expert-curated science evals.

title: LLMs Outperform Experts on Challenging Biology Benchmarks
why: Cautions on biology-benchmark saturation and eval errors — directly motivates SciPredict's calibration framing.

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
why: The prompting method that makes 'thinking traces' exist — the very substrate MoReBench grades; most-cited reference.

title: Aligning AI With Shared Human Values
why: The ETHICS benchmark — the outcome-focused moral-values eval MoReBench reasons 'beyond' by scoring process.

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
why: The capability predictor MoReBench shows breaks down: bigger isn't better at moral reasoning.

title: The Moral Machine experiment
why: Landmark global study of human moral preferences over machine decisions; foundational moral-ML reference.

title: Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think: Unfaithful Explanations in Chain-of-Thought Prompting
why: CoT can be unfaithful — directly bears on whether graded thinking traces reflect a model's real reasoning.

title: MCP-Universe: Benchmarking Large Language Models with Real-World Model Context Protocol Servers
why: The closest sibling MCP benchmark — it named the 'unknown-tools' challenge that motivates MCP-Atlas's distractor design; most-cited MCP eval.

title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
why: The LLM-as-judge methodology MCP-Atlas's three-judge protocol follows; foundational reference for model-graded evaluation.

title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs
why: The canonical large-scale tool-learning benchmark; the API-orchestration lineage MCP-Atlas extends to real MCP servers.

title: τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
why: Influential tool-agent-user interaction benchmark (retail/airline) and the basis of τ2-Bench; a key tool-use eval baseline.

title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
why: Widely-used multi-tool assistant benchmark (web search + code) testing planning, error recovery, and orchestration.

title: LegalBench: A Collaboratively Built Benchmark for Measuring Legal Reasoning in Large Language Models
why: The foundational collaboratively-built legal-reasoning benchmark; PRBench's Legal split compares against it directly and mirrors its Rule Application axis.

title: FinanceBench: A New Benchmark for Financial Question Answering
why: Large, widely-cited financial-QA benchmark; the open-book finance eval PRBench contrasts its open-ended rubric tasks against.

title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
why: OpenAI's economically-valuable-tasks eval; the flagship framing PRBench builds on when annotating tasks for downstream economic impact.

title: ProfBench: Multi-Domain Rubrics requiring Professional Knowledge to Answer and Judge
why: Closest sibling: a multi-domain professional rubric benchmark (2,448 criteria) that PRBench is 'an order of magnitude larger' than.

title: The AI Productivity Index (APEX)
why: A professional rubric benchmark (5,818 criteria over 200 tasks) and a direct Table-4 comparison point for PRBench's scale and methodology.

title: BrowseComp: A Simple Yet Challenging Benchmark for Browsing Agents
why: OpenAI's hard browsing-agent benchmark (1,200+ multi-hop web questions); the retrieval-accuracy lineage ResearchRubrics contrasts its open-ended synthesis tasks against.

title: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents
why: The closest DR sibling — 100 PhD-level report tasks — whose LLM-generated rubrics and reference-overlap metrics ResearchRubrics is explicitly built to avoid.

title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
why: The MAST failure taxonomy ResearchRubrics maps its reasoning-action-mismatch and task-disobedience failures onto.

title: Towards an AI co-scientist
why: DeepMind's multi-agent hypothesis-generating co-scientist; the frontier of autonomous research agents ResearchRubrics aims to evaluate.

title: Mind2Web 2: Evaluating Agentic Search with Agent-as-a-Judge
why: Agent-as-a-Judge benchmark for agentic search (50 rubrics/task); a key Table-1 comparison point ResearchRubrics positions against.

title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?
why: OpenAI's freelance-SWE economic benchmark ($1M of real Upwork tasks) — the closest sibling to RLI's economic-value framing, scoped to software.

title: OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments
why: The canonical real-computer-environment agent benchmark; the computer-use lineage RLI's CUA scaffold builds on.

title: RE-Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI R&D Capabilities of Language Model Agents against Human Experts
why: METR's head-to-head of agents vs human experts on frontier AI R&D tasks — a human-anchored capability benchmark like RLI.

title: HCAST: Human-Calibrated Autonomy Software Tasks
why: METR's human-time-calibrated autonomy benchmark — directly compared against RLI in Figure 6 and central to the time-horizon framing of agent capability.

title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
why: Foundational realistic web-agent environment; a core computer/web-use benchmark RLI positions its broader remote-work scope against.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Rubric Anchors
why: The rubric-RL work OnlineRubrics builds on and the source of the 'self-praising' reward-hacking pattern that motivates eliciting criteria online.

title: Checklists Are Better Than Reward Models For Aligning Language Models
why: The 'Universal Requirements' baseline — fixed checklists for alignment — that OnlineRubrics' sample-grounded online criteria are shown to beat.

title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
why: Introduces GRPO, the policy-gradient algorithm OnlineRubrics trains with; one of its most-cited references.

title: DeepSeek-R1 Incentivizes Reasoning in LLMs through Reinforcement Learning
why: Foundational RLVR / R1-Zero result and the motivation that opens the paper: policies trained directly via RL, skipping SFT.

title: R3: Robust Rubric-Agnostic Reward Models
why: Trains a single robust reward model that generalizes across domains using diverse rubrics — a rubric-as-reward-model contrast to OnlineRubrics' online augmentation.
